summary: Joel Spolsky announces that Fog Creek Software is hiring due to strong sales growth. He reflects on his previous hiring strategy of posting on job boards like Monster and Craigslist, which produced massive piles of unqualified applicants. He's shifting to a new approach: maintaining permanent job listings on the Fog Creek website to attract a steadier stream of more qualified candidates. He also announces a search for a filmmaker to document the software development process over the summer.
key_points: Fog Creek's sales growth has outpaced their ability to keep up, forcing a shift from rare, quiet hiring to active recruitment
Traditional job boards like Monster and Craigslist produce high volume but low quality applicant pools
A company's own career page may attract more targeted, qualified candidates than generic job boards
Fog Creek plans to create a documentary about the software development process
money_quotes: Until now we've been hiring rarely and quietly, but lately our sales are so strong we can't quite keep up.
My old theory of hiring was to post a job listing on Monster or Craigslist and then sort through the massive pile of unqualified applicants in hopes of finding the needle in the haystack.

In the future I'm going to try putting up semi-permanent job listings for all the kinds of people we might hire on the Fog Creek website and see if that gets us a slower trickle of more qualified job applicants.
key_insight: Casting a wide net on generic job boards produces quantity over quality; a company's own career page can serve as a better filter for attracting qualified candidates who already know and care about your work.
